I wanted to understand the stories and the flow of ideas
in this community.
So I tried semantic clustering first.
You embed all the tweets.
You try to cluster them.
And you get groups of tweets that are a little bit too broad.
So an example is you might end up with a cluster of just broadly
meditation-y themed things.
But I want stories.
I want threads of narrative that are much more specific than that.
And I examined the meditation cluster.
And found one really cool story.
I think some Vietnamese Dharma person has the saying, like, the next Buddha is a Sangha.
And over time, people kind of remixed this and all the Dharma-oriented people made them, yeah, you know, like, the next Buddha is an internet community.
And that's a story that you can tell, that I found in the data.
But semantic clustering didn't work that well to do it in an automated, exhaustive way, right?
So I tried to find strands, which worked better.
So I'm going to tell you a little bit about strands.
They worked better.
We took this tweet about community building, the original one, and we found tweets causally downstream of it and tweets topically upstream of it.
Like I was saying, strands are centered on one tweet, like causal light cones, downstream, upstream, backwards and forwards in time.
This is my working diagram, but it's what it says on the tin.
And, oh yeah, I guess I could explain.
It's like, you have a tweet, it gets quoted a bunch of times in the future, or it gets replied to, and all of those are kind of causally
upstream, right?
And you could implement the causally upstream in many ways.
In my case, I just did semantic similarity, and it's close enough.
We still get pretty cool results.

Well, bangers, of course.

norms that people end up adopting over time.
During COVID, lots of kind of rationalist-y, dharma-y flavored people join Twitter.
They find Visa's posting norms and it creates this kind of really generative intellectual scene
where people who are previously pretty rationalist-minded, pretty left brain, end up discovering all
of these spiritual traditions and therapy modalities and embodied practices.
And that was kind of the golden age, but things keep going.

in epistemic rigor, you know, like have the tools of rationality, like Bayesian thinking
and so on and systems thinking, look at these old traditions and kind of try them out and explain
them in rationalist terms, and that makes them more accessible to new people who are kind
of like more left brain emphasis.
Having access to these practices, they end up, you know, having more nervous system capacity

Yeah, so we should jam on it.
Any other questions?
You talked sort of briefly about, like, assembling threads from, like, posts.
I was wondering if you could, like, elaborate a bit more on that process.
I felt like it was kind of unclear to me how you take one post and turn it into this whole, you know, how do you find, like, posts that form some sort of story?
Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
So I think the core insight-- I think the core insight is that, first, we pick the central posts well, because they're posts that are very likely to have mattered.
And they have lots of quote tweets.
They're cited lots of times, right?
And then the actual way we build the strands is-- so you get the post.
Then you take all the times it was quoted, all the posts that quote the original one.
Right?
Then we take all of those.
We do semantic search on them and find, you know, the 50, if you want, you know, most semantically similar posts,
so that we have some chance of getting past posts, posts from the past.
Because otherwise, it would be all in the future.
Then for each of those, we get all their threads, right?
Like, all the reply trees.
And that gives us something that looks kind of like this.
And did I-- at first, when you asked your question, I thought you were asking about how to stitch the strands together.
But was that not the question?
